diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Explosive paroxysmal sneezing is a hallmark of the early-phase allergic response, especially with an identifiable allergen trigger.
Sneezing also occurs with viral URTI but is usually less explosive and paroxysmal.

symptoms.symptom_name: Profuse clear watery rhinorrhoea then bilateral nasal obstruction

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Clear watery discharge followed by mucosal-oedema obstruction is typical of allergic rhinitis; purulent/coloured discharge would favour infection.
Early viral rhinitis can also be watery; the discriminating feature is the associated itch and explosive onset.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intense pruritus of nose, palate and throat with itchy watery eyes

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Prominent nasal/palatal/ocular itch is the single most discriminating feature separating allergic rhinitis from viral rhinitis — itch points strongly to an IgE-mediated mechanism.
Prominent conjunctival and palatal pruritus is not a feature of viral URTI and argues against it as the primary cause.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sudden onset shortly after dust exposure during renovations

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A clear temporal link to an aeroallergen exposure (house/renovation dust, dust mite) is strong evidence for an allergic trigger.

symptoms.symptom_name: Subjective sensation of suffocation / total nasal blockage with panic

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Complete nasal occlusion from turbinate oedema produces a distressing sense of not being able to breathe, compounded by anxiety — but reflects upper-nasal obstruction, not lower-airway compromise.
A 'can't breathe' complaint is non-specific: it mandates airway screening but, in isolation with normal air entry, does not itself indicate anaphylaxis.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dry tickly cough from postnasal drip

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Postnasal drip from rhinorrhoea commonly causes a non-productive cough; supportive but non-specific.
Absence of wheeze/stridor argues strongly against lower-airway or laryngeal involvement, a required element to exclude anaphylaxis with respiratory compromise.
No wheeze and no lower-airway symptoms argue against a concurrent asthma exacerbation.

symptoms.symptom_name: No lip/tongue swelling and no dysphagia (pertinent negative)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of angioedema (lip/tongue swelling) and dysphagia excludes an impending upper-airway obstruction from angioedema — the key mimic to rule out.
Absence of widespread urticaria argues against a systemic allergic reaction; with the other negatives it confirms a localised allergic response.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pale, bluish, boggy nasal turbinates with copious clear secretions on anterior rhinoscopy

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Pale, bluish, boggy turbinates with watery secretions are the classic rhinoscopic signature of allergic rhinitis (versus the erythematous mucosa of viral rhinitis). Clinician-only finding — sealed; cannot be obtained via telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Chest clear to auscultation, equal air entry, no wheeze

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Definitively clear lung fields with equal air entry exclude bronchospasm — clinician auscultation, sealed. The AI Doctor must instead infer normal lower-airway function from the patient speaking in full sentences and denying wheeze.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal vital signs including SpO2 99% and afebrile

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal oxygen saturation, heart rate, blood pressure and temperature indicate no objective respiratory or circulatory compromise. These are device/clinician measurements the patient does not have — sealed; the AI Doctor cannot verify them remotely.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Paroxysmal sneezing + watery rhinorrhoea + nasal/palatal/ocular pruritus + a clear aeroallergen trigger together form a high-probability allergic rhinitis constellation; the itch is the strongest single discriminator from viral rhinitis.

The combination of no wheeze/stridor, no lip/tongue swelling or dysphagia, and no urticaria excludes anaphylaxis/angioedema and is what licenses low-tier management of an alarming-sounding presentation.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Allergen testing (skin-prick or serum specific IgE) — outpatient, only if symptoms become chronic/recurrent
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms the specific aeroallergen and guides avoidance/immunotherapy if symptoms persist. Not required for acute management and not obtainable via telehealth in real time.
No role in uncomplicated allergic rhinitis; ordering them represents over-investigation of a clinical diagnosis.
Not indicated in acute allergic rhinitis; reserved for suspected complicated/chronic rhinosinusitis or polyposis not responding to treatment. Recommending it acutely is over-complication.
